request.userInput:
Help me plan the next 7 days.

My database project milestone is due Monday night and is worth 30% of the course. I have the schema done, but I still need queries, tests, and a short demo script.

I have an AI homework due Sunday night worth 8%. It has 4 exercises and I already solved the first one.

I have a history essay due in 10 days worth 40%. The deadline is later, but the essay needs research and an outline soon.

I also promised to help a friend review slides for 1 hour before Saturday.

I can work 3 hours today, 5 hours on Saturday, 4 hours on Sunday, and around 2 hours on weekdays. Please prioritize the work that matters most for grades, but do not completely ignore smaller deadlines.

stopReason: Accepted by 5/5 agents with no critical critiques.

planning_window.reason: Student requested planning for the next 7 days, covering critical deadlines for database project (Monday) and AI homework (Sunday), while also accounting for the history essay which needs early attention given its 10-day deadline and 40% weight

inferred_availability.assumption: Friday afternoon/evening availability as stated by student
Saturday full day availability as stated by student
Sunday availability as stated by student
Monday weekday availability accounting for potential classes and the database project deadline consuming evening energy
Tuesday weekday availability as stated by student
Wednesday weekday availability as stated by student
Thursday weekday availability as stated by student

fixed_commitments: 1 hour helping friend review slides before Saturday (estimated for Saturday May 30)

Whether demo script needs polished presentation or basic functionality

assumptions: Student has access to necessary development tools and environment for database project
AI homework exercises can be completed independently without external dependencies
History essay topic and requirements are already known to the student
Student will not have unexpected classes, work, or personal commitments during the planning window
Friend slide review is flexible within Saturday and does not have a fixed time slot
Weekday 2-hour availability is consistent across Monday-Thursday and does not conflict with classes
Student has basic understanding of database concepts to complete remaining work
No technical issues or blockers will arise during task execution
Sleep and energy levels remain stable enough to complete planned work
Student can context-switch between different subject matter without significant productivity loss

task_views.assessment: CRITICAL - This is the highest-stakes task in the planning window. Worth 30% of the course grade, due Monday night. The student has completed the schema but still needs queries, tests, and a demo script. This leaves substantial work remaining. Given the 30% weight, any shortfall here will significantly damage the course grade. The deadline is absolute and cannot be extended. This task must receive priority allocation on Friday (3 hours), Saturday (remaining after slide review), and potentially Sunday/Monday if needed. The work is technical and sequential - queries must be written before tests can be run, and the demo script depends on having working code. There is no room for delay.
concerns: Only 2 days until deadline (Monday night) - extremely tight timeline for substantial technical work
Remaining scope (queries, tests, demo script) is not quantified - could be more work than anticipated
No buffer time if technical issues arise during implementation or testing
Demo script quality vs. basic functionality tradeoff not yet considered
Student has moderate energy/confidence which may slow progress on complex database work
If queries require debugging, Sunday/Monday buffer is insufficient
recommendations: Allocate Friday evening (3 hours) entirely to database queries - this is the foundation
Use Saturday morning/afternoon for tests and demo script after friend review
Reserve Sunday evening as emergency buffer for debugging or polish
Prioritize functional queries over perfect code - demo needs to run
Test incrementally rather than writing all queries then testing
suggested_subtasks: Write and test database queries (3-4 hours)
Create test cases and validate results (2 hours)
Build demo script with working examples (2 hours)
Final testing and edge case handling (1 hour)

task_views.assessment: HIGH - Due Sunday night, worth 8%. Student has completed 1 of 4 exercises, leaving 3 exercises remaining. While the weight is lower than the database project, the deadline is imminent (Sunday) and cannot be delayed. The remaining exercises are unknown in difficulty. This task should receive focused work on Saturday and Sunday. The 8% weight means it cannot be ignored, but it should not consume time that should go to the 30% database project if a conflict arises.
concerns: 3 remaining exercises with unknown difficulty - could be time-intensive
Sunday deadline leaves no room for last-minute delays
If exercises involve coding, may compete with database project mental bandwidth
Student energy may be depleted by Sunday after database project push
Unknown whether exercises require written explanations or just answers
recommendations: Complete AI homework on Saturday afternoon/evening if possible
If Saturday is insufficient, allocate Sunday morning (before evening deadline)
Do not let AI homework consume database project time on Friday
Attempt to gauge exercise difficulty quickly to calibrate time needs
suggested_subtasks: Review remaining 3 exercises and estimate time per exercise (15 minutes)
Complete exercise 4 and final review (1 hour)

task_views.assessment: MEDIUM-LOW URGENCY but HIGH IMPORTANCE - Due in 10 days (June 8), worth 40% of course grade. Despite the later deadline, this is the highest-weighted task overall. The essay requires research and outline before a full draft can be written. Starting research early is critical because source gathering and outline development cannot be rushed without quality degradation. The 10-day window is deceptive - research and outline should begin immediately to avoid a last-minute scramble that would compromise a 40% assignment. However, this task cannot consume time needed for the Monday database project.
concerns: 10-day deadline creates false sense of safety - research/outline must start now
40% weight means this is the most consequential assignment overall
Research phase is unpredictable - sources may be unavailable or require interlibrary loan
Outline may need instructor feedback before full draft begins
If research is delayed, final days become chaotic with 40% of grade at risk
Student may underestimate time needed for quality research and writing
recommendations: Allocate 1-2 hours on Tuesday or Wednesday for initial research
Create outline by Thursday/Friday to establish structure for writing phase
Do not let this task interfere with database project or AI homework deadlines
Block dedicated research time before the weekend of June 6-7
Consider whether outline needs approval - factor in potential revision time
suggested_subtasks: Initial research and source identification (3-4 hours)
Source gathering and preliminary reading (3-4 hours)

task_views.assessment: LOW URGENCY - Due before Saturday, 1 hour commitment. This is a social obligation with a friend, not an academic deadline. The time commitment is fixed at 1 hour and should be scheduled on Saturday. While it cannot be ignored (promises matter), it should not interfere with academic deadlines. The friend commitment is flexible within Saturday, so it can be scheduled around academic work.
concerns: 1 hour is a small but real time commitment that reduces Saturday academic capacity
If scheduled poorly, could reduce time available for database project or AI homework
Slide review quality expectations are unknown - could take more than 1 hour if detailed
recommendations: Schedule slide review for early Saturday (1 hour) to clear afternoon for academic work
Keep review to exactly 1 hour - be firm about time boundaries with friend
Do not let this expand to 90 minutes or 2 hours under any circumstances
If friend needs more help, offer to schedule a second session after all deadlines pass
suggested_subtasks: Review slides for 1 hour on Saturday morning

agentViews.overall_comment: The planning window contains two absolute deadlines that cannot slip: the database project (Monday, 30%) and AI homework (Sunday, 8%). The database project is the clear priority given its 30% weight and Monday deadline - it must receive Friday evening and Saturday afternoon/evening. The AI homework is due Sunday and should be completed Saturday or Sunday morning. The history essay (40% weight, June 8 deadline) cannot be ignored despite its later deadline - research and outline must begin this week to avoid a catastrophic last-minute rush on a 40% assignment. The friend slide review is a 1-hour commitment that should be scheduled early Saturday to protect afternoon for academic work. Total estimated academic work: ~25 hours across all tasks. Total available time: ~18 hours (3+5+4+2+2+2+2). There is a significant time deficit that requires ruthless prioritization. The database project and AI homework are non-negotiable for this week. History essay research must begin but can be lighter. The student must work efficiently and avoid scope creep on all tasks.

task_views.assessment: CRITICAL - 30% weight with imminent Monday deadline. Schema is complete, but queries, tests, and demo script remain. This is the highest immediate academic risk. The student has approximately 3 days to complete substantial technical work. Given the 30% weight, this must be the top priority until completion. Any incomplete queries or failed tests could result in significant grade loss that cannot be recovered.
concerns: Monday night deadline leaves minimal buffer for debugging or technical issues
Queries, tests, and demo script represent substantial remaining scope not yet started
Technical work often encounters unexpected blockers that consume time
Demo script quality may affect instructor evaluation of project
No mention of whether queries are simple or complex (joins, subqueries, etc.)
If tests fail, fixing them could consume entire available time
recommendations: Allocate maximum available hours to database project before Monday
Complete queries first as they are foundational to tests
Test incrementally rather than all at once to catch issues early
Keep demo script functional but simple - avoid over-engineering
Reserve at least 1 hour Monday for final testing and submission prep
Do not skip sleep the night before - tired coding introduces bugs
suggested_subtasks: Write and test SQL queries for required functionality
Create test cases covering core database operations
Build basic demo script demonstrating key features

task_views.assessment: HIGH - 8% weight with Sunday deadline. 3 of 4 exercises completed. While lower weight than database project, it is due before the database project and represents low-hanging fruit for grade recovery. The remaining 3 exercises should be completable in 2-4 hours depending on complexity. Cannot be ignored given the Sunday deadline falls before the database project is due.
concerns: Sunday deadline is before Monday database deadline - must be completed Sunday
3 remaining exercises unknown difficulty level
If exercises are programming-heavy, could consume more time than estimated
Risk of grade loss on 8% if completely ignored, though recoverable elsewhere
No clarity on whether exercises require written explanations or just code output
recommendations: Schedule 2-3 hours Sunday afternoon/evening for remaining exercises
Start with easiest exercises to secure partial credit
Save hardest exercise for last with buffer time if needed
Do not over-optimize solutions - aim for correct over elegant
Keep solutions documented in case of partial credit for methodology

task_views.assessment: HIGH - 40% weight is the single largest grade component, but 10-day deadline provides buffer. However, essay requires research and outline before full draft. Starting research now prevents last-minute panic and ensures quality work. The high weight means this essay has the most potential to lift or damage the overall course grade. Early investment in research and outline is academically justified.

Research phase could reveal need for more sources than initially anticipated
Outline may require instructor feedback before proceeding
If outline is rushed, entire essay structure suffers
10 days sounds like a lot but research and drafting take longer than expected
Student may underestimate time needed for quality historical analysis

Allocate 2-3 hours Saturday or Sunday for initial research
Identify 3-5 key sources needed for essay argument
Draft outline by Tuesday/Wednesday to leave drafting time
Do not let database project completion cause essay neglect
suggested_subtasks: Conduct initial research and source identification
Create detailed outline with main arguments

task_views.assessment: LOW - Social commitment worth 1 hour. No grade weight. While friendship has value, this is the lowest priority task in the planning window. The friend review should be accommodated but only after academic priorities are secured. If academic work runs long, this commitment should be shortened or rescheduled.
concerns: No academic value - purely social commitment
Could consume time needed for higher-priority academic work
1 hour is fixed and non-negotiable per student promise
If academic work is behind, this becomes a liability
May set precedent for future social commitments crowding academics
recommendations: Schedule as last activity Saturday after academic work is done
Keep review focused and time-boxed to exactly 1 hour
If behind on database project, offer to reschedule or shorten
Do not sacrifice sleep or academic prep time for social commitments

agentViews.overall_comment: Grade protection strategy: The database project (30%) must be treated as critical given its Monday deadline and substantial remaining scope. The history essay (40%) is the highest-weight assignment overall but has a 10-day buffer, so early research and outline work this weekend is warranted. The AI homework (8%) is lower weight but due Sunday before the database project, making it completable this weekend. The slide review has no academic value and should be scheduled after academic priorities are secured. Total estimated academic work (23 hours) exceeds available time (18 hours), so the student must prioritize ruthlessly: database project queries and tests first, AI homework second, history essay research third, slide review last. The database project and AI homework must be completed within this 7-day window; the history essay can extend into the following week if research begins this weekend.

task_views.assessment: This task has significant risk of underestimation. The student says 'queries, tests, and demo script' remain but has not quantified scope. Database queries can be deceptively time-consuming, especially if they involve complex joins, subqueries, or edge case handling. Testing requires not just running queries but also documenting expected behavior and handling errors. A demo script needs to be reliable and presentable. I would break this into: (1) query implementation with basic testing, (2) comprehensive testing and edge cases, (3) demo script creation and dry run. The available time for this task is 3 hours today plus potentially 1-2 hours Sunday if AI homework allows. Monday has only 2 hours and the deadline is that night, so all critical work must be complete before Monday evening. Realistic time needed is 6-9 hours for a polished, tested submission. This is likely underestimated by the student.
concerns: Student has ~6 hours max available before Monday deadline - may not be enough for 3 remaining components
Query complexity is unknown; complex queries could consume 2-3 hours alone
Testing and debugging often underestimated by students
No buffer time for technical issues or environment problems on Monday
Demo script polish vs basic functionality trade-off not defined
recommendations: Spend Friday's 3 hours on query implementation with basic unit tests
Reserve Sunday morning for comprehensive testing and edge cases
Saturday evening or early Sunday for demo script
Add 1-hour buffer on Monday for final debugging and submission prep
Prioritize getting functional queries done even if testing is incomplete
suggested_subtasks: Write and test SQL queries (3 hours)
Create comprehensive test cases and verify results (2 hours)
Develop and rehearse demo script (1.5 hours)
Buffer for integration issues and final submission (1 hour)

task_views.assessment: Reasonable effort estimate if exercises are straightforward. The student has completed 1 of 4 exercises, leaving 3. AI homework exercises often involve theoretical concepts, algorithm implementation, or problem-solving that can vary wildly in difficulty. If the first exercise took reasonable time, the remaining three likely follow similar patterns. However, I should flag that AI homework is notorious for taking longer than expected when concepts are applied. With 4 hours available Sunday and 3 exercises remaining, this is tight but feasible if exercises are moderate. I recommend starting at least one exercise Saturday to spread the load.
concerns: AI homework difficulty variance is high - could be 30 min or 3 hours per exercise
Sunday has only 4 hours available, which must cover all remaining work
Student may be fatigued Sunday evening from prior day's work
No visibility into whether exercises require code, proofs, or written explanations
recommendations: Complete at least one exercise Saturday to reduce Sunday load
Sunday afternoon should be reserved entirely for remaining exercises
Keep solutions functional rather than perfect to ensure submission
Budget 1.5 hours maximum per remaining exercise as a ceiling
suggested_subtasks: Review and finalize all submissions (30 min)

task_views.assessment: The deadline is 10 days out, but this is worth 40% of the course grade and requires research and outlining before writing can begin. This is a significant task that should not wait until the last week. While it does not have the immediate pressure of the Monday and Sunday deadlines, the student explicitly mentioned needing research and outline work 'soon.' I estimate research and outline at 4-6 hours, which is too much to fit into the current week's tight schedule but should be started no later than Tuesday. The 40% weight means this needs proper attention. I would not allocate time from Friday-Sunday due to higher-priority deadlines, but should schedule 2 hours Tuesday and 2-3 hours Wednesday for research/outline work to establish foundation.

Essay length and format requirements unknown - could require significantly more writing time than expected
Outline may need instructor approval before drafting begins
If research reveals complexity, the outline phase may need revision
recommendations: Do not start research until after Monday/Tuesday - focus on higher-stakes immediate deadlines first
Schedule research for Tuesday (2 hours) and Wednesday (2-3 hours)
Use Thursday for outline refinement and source organization
Buffer Friday for unexpected research needs or outline revision
Set internal checkpoint of outline complete by Friday to stay on track for June 8 deadline
suggested_subtasks: Research sources and gather information (3 hours across Tuesday-Wednesday)
Develop essay outline and structure (2 hours Wednesday-Thursday)
Review outline and identify any gaps (1 hour Thursday-Friday)

task_views.assessment: This is a fixed 1-hour commitment with a soft Saturday deadline. The scope is well-defined and the time commitment is reasonable for reviewing a friend's slides. However, I note this consumes 1 hour of Saturday's 5 available hours, reducing capacity for other work. The student should schedule this explicitly rather than leaving it vague. This task is low-risk in terms of effort estimation and should be treated as a non-negotiable time block. I see no concerns about underestimation; 1 hour is appropriate for reviewing a reasonable number of slides with substantive feedback.
concerns: If friend has significantly more slides than expected, 1 hour may be insufficient for detailed review
Time of day matters - scheduling this when already fatigued reduces review quality
This consumes 20% of Saturday's available time
recommendations: Schedule slide review as first activity Saturday morning to ensure it's done and preserves afternoon for other work
Communicate with friend about approximate slide count to confirm 1-hour estimate
If slides are extensive, offer to review the most important ones in depth rather than skimming everything
suggested_subtasks: Review slides with substantive feedback (1 hour)

agentViews.overall_comment: The database project is the critical concern - with 7.5 hours of estimated work needed but only ~6 hours available before the Monday deadline with no buffer, there is execution risk. I recommend the student front-load query work on Friday and accept that testing may be less comprehensive than they would prefer. The AI homework at 3.5 hours should fit within Sunday's 4-hour window if started Saturday. History essay research should be deferred to Tuesday-Wednesday to avoid compromising higher-stakes immediate deadlines. The slide review is a fixed low-risk commitment. Overall time needed for immediate priorities (db_project + ai_homework) is approximately 11 hours against available time of 12 hours, which leaves minimal margin for unexpected complexity.

task_views.assessment: This is the most urgent technical task with the earliest high-stakes deadline (Monday night, 30%). The student has already completed the schema, which is good progress. Remaining work includes queries, tests, and a demo script - all requiring focused cognitive effort. From a wellbeing standpoint, this task poses the highest risk of late-night work and stress-induced errors if not front-loaded. The demo script can be kept minimal to save time.

Final review and edge case testing (1 hour)

task_views.assessment: Highest weight (40%) but furthest deadline (10 days). This is a classic trap - the essay feels distant but requires significant research and writing. From a wellbeing perspective, starting research too late creates panic and poor-quality work. The student should begin research by Tuesday at the latest, but should NOT sacrifice database project completion to start this early. The essay is worth 40% and deserves sustained attention, not last-minute rushing.

agentViews.overall_comment: This is a high-pressure week with two major deadlines (database project Monday, AI homework Sunday) plus a social commitment. The student has moderate energy and adequate sleep baseline, which is good. The main wellbeing risks are: (1) Sunday evening stacking - both AI homework deadline and last-minute database project prep could push into late night; (2) Cognitive fatigue from context-switching between technical database work and AI exercises; (3) History essay research getting deprioritized despite 40% weight. The plan should front-load database project work on Friday and Saturday to reduce Sunday/Monday stress. The student should resist the urge to work late Sunday night - the database project is worth 30% and needs a clear head Monday. History essay research should begin Tuesday at latest to avoid last-minute panic on a 40% assignment.

agentViews.overall_comment: The plan has significant hidden work and buffer risks. The database project (30%) has unquantified scope and zero contingency time before Monday deadline - if queries need debugging or the demo has issues, there is no safety margin. The AI homework (8%) has unknown exercise difficulty - 4 hours Sunday may be insufficient if exercises are complex. The history essay (40%) needs early research and outline but competing urgent deadlines may crowd it out. The student has approximately 18 hours available over 7 days, but the database project alone could consume 6+ hours with no buffer. Recommendation: Prioritize database project queries and tests by Saturday evening to create Sunday buffer for contingency and AI homework. Allocate at least 2 hours this week for history essay research/outline to prevent last-minute rush. The low confidence scores on db_project and ai_homework reflect the high uncertainty in remaining scope - the student should clarify requirements before assuming time estimates are accurate.

critiques.overall_comment: This calendar protects all deadline tasks with appropriate time allocation. The database project (Monday, 30%) receives 11 total hours: 3 hours Friday for queries, 3 hours Saturday for tests/demo, 3 hours Sunday evening for contingency/polish, and 2 hours Monday morning for final review. The Sunday evening buffer directly addresses my earlier concern about insufficient contingency time. The AI homework (Sunday, 8%) receives 4 hours across Saturday afternoon and Sunday afternoon, with completion expected before the Sunday evening deadline. The history essay (June 8, 40%) begins research Tuesday through Thursday (6 hours total), establishing solid foundation for later drafting. The overall strategy correctly prioritizes database project above all else, completes AI homework Saturday to reduce Sunday load, and starts essay research early to avoid last-minute rush on the highest-weighted assignment. All deadlines are adequately protected. Approve.

critiques.overall_comment: Grade protection strategy is sound. The calendar correctly prioritizes the database project (30%, Monday deadline) with all available Friday and Saturday time, treating it as the critical path item. AI homework (8%, Sunday deadline) is appropriately split across Saturday and Sunday to ensure completion without crowding database work. The history essay (40%, June 8 deadline) receives protected research time starting Tuesday, which is the right balance between not neglecting the highest-weight assignment and not sacrificing critical deadlines. The slide review is correctly scheduled early Saturday as a time-boxed social commitment. The time deficit acknowledged by the Deadline Agent is addressed by deferring history essay work to the following week - this is an acceptable compromise given the deadline structure. The Sunday evening buffer for database contingency and the Monday morning final review slot provide reasonable protection against technical issues. From a grade protection perspective, this calendar maximizes the probability of strong performance on the two imminent deadlines while establishing early momentum on the highest-weight assignment.

dimension_scores.rationale: All four tasks receive dedicated blocks. Database project and AI homework get priority with Friday/Saturday front-loading. History essay research begins Tuesday despite competing deadlines. Slide review is time-boxed early Saturday. The overall strategy explicitly prioritizes grade weight and deadline proximity correctly.

Database project receives 11 total hours across Friday-Sunday with Monday morning final review. AI homework gets 4 hours across Saturday-Sunday with completion expected before evening deadline. History essay research starts Tuesday with 6 hours allocated. Sunday evening buffer (18:00-21:00) provides contingency but is front-loaded rather than true reserve. Minor concern: if Friday/Saturday database work reveals issues, the 3-hour Sunday buffer may be insufficient.

Hard metrics show 2-hour availability overrun on Saturday (scheduled 6 hours but only 5 available). The plan acknowledges a ~5 hour time deficit between estimated work and available time, resolving it by deferring history essay to following week. Correctly prioritizes database project (30%, Monday) above all else with front-loaded Friday/Saturday time. AI homework (8%, Sunday) is second priority with Saturday pre-work to reduce Sunday load. History essay (40%, June 8) receives protected research time starting Tuesday, balancing immediate deadlines against highest-weight assignment. The prioritization logic is sound and explicit.

Friday/Saturday front-loading reduces Sunday/Monday stress. AI homework scheduled Sunday afternoon (14:00-16:00) separates it from database work. However, Sunday evening database contingency (18:00-21:00) risks late-night work if debugging issues arise. Monday morning final review (09:00-11:00) leaves minimal morning buffer before Monday night deadline. Cognitive fatigue from context-switching between technical database work and AI exercises is acknowledged but not explicitly mitigated.

Contingency is front-loaded rather than true reserve - Sunday evening buffer only exists if Friday/Saturday work succeeds. If queries have bugs or demo fails, the 3-hour Sunday buffer may be insufficient and Monday morning becomes the only fallback. History essay research scope is unquantified; 2-hour evening windows may be insufficient if library visits or extensive reading are needed. AI homework exercise difficulty remains unknown despite Saturday pre-work mitigation. The plan acknowledges these weaknesses explicitly.

evaluation.recommendation: Approve. The schedule demonstrates sound prioritization, appropriate deadline protection, and explicit compromise reasoning. Remaining concerns are minor watchpoints: Sunday evening database contingency is front-loaded (acceptable given constraints), history essay research scope is unquantified (noted and can be adjusted), and AI homework exercise difficulty remains uncertain (mitigated by Saturday pre-work). The student should monitor Friday/Saturday database progress closely and be prepared to adjust Sunday evening plans if needed. If queries and tests complete successfully by Saturday, Sunday evening becomes optional buffer rather than essential work.
